course: CMU 21-254: Linear Algebra and Vector Calculus for Engineers
title: Matrices, Transposes, Multiplication, and Special Matrices
audience: SI leader teaching live on a whiteboard
toneRule: Keep explanations very simple, verbal, and board-friendly. Avoid textbook phrasing unless needed.
sessionGoal: Students should be able to multiply matrices, check dimensions, use transposes correctly, and recognize simple matrix power patterns.
quickStartIfUnprepared: Start by writing the dimension rule: (m×n)(n×p) gives m×p.
Say: matrix multiplication is row dot column.
Warn that matrix multiplication is usually not commutative: AB and BA can be different or have different sizes.
Do Problem 1 for multiplication and dimensions.
Do Problem 2 for transpose order reversal.
Do Problem 3 if time remains for powers and the identity matrix.
sayThisFirst: A matrix is a rectangular table of numbers.
Matrix multiplication works only when the inner dimensions match.
Each entry of AB comes from one row of A dotted with one column of B.
Order matters: AB is not usually the same as BA.
Transpose means flip rows and columns.
For products, transpose reverses the order: \((AB)^T=B^TA^T\).

boardPlan.whatToSay: Today is mostly about order. Check dimensions before doing any arithmetic.
Make students say the sizes first. Do not let them start multiplying until the dimensions make sense.
Transpose a product by reversing the order. This is a common place to make a small but important mistake.
Once you see A squared is the identity, stop multiplying over and over. Use the pattern.
Ask: what do we check before multiplying? What happens to order when transposing a product?

keyFormulas.name: Matrix multiplication dimensions
keyFormulas.plainEnglish: The inner dimensions must match. The outer dimensions give the answer size.

keyFormulas.name: Entry of a product
keyFormulas.plainEnglish: Row i of A dotted with column j of B.

keyFormulas.name: Transpose of a product
keyFormulas.plainEnglish: Flip each matrix and reverse the order.

keyFormulas.name: Identity matrix
keyFormulas.plainEnglish: Multiplying by the identity leaves a matrix unchanged, when the sizes work.

commonMistakes: Trying to multiply matrices without checking dimensions first.
Forgetting that AB and BA can have different sizes.
Computing an entry of AB using column times column instead of row times column.
Forgetting that \(A^2\) means \(AA\), not squaring each entry.

notationWarnings.why: Transpose reverses the order of multiplication.
Matrix powers mean repeated matrix multiplication.

problems.title: Matrix multiplication: check dimensions first
problems.studentPromptLatex: Let \(A=\begin{bmatrix}1&-2&0\\3&1&4\end{bmatrix}\) and \(B=\begin{bmatrix}2&-1\\0&5\\3&2\end{bmatrix}\). Compute \(AB\) and \(BA\), if they are defined. Are they equal?
problems.whatToSayBefore: Before multiplying, ask for the size of each matrix. A is 2×3 and B is 3×2.

visualSpec.purpose: Show that dimensions decide whether a product exists, and one product entry comes from row dot column.
dimensionFacts: AB is defined and has size 2×2.
BA is defined and has size 3×3.
AB and BA cannot be equal here because they do not even have the same size.
boardSketchInstructions: Write A as a 2×3 block and B as a 3×2 block.
Highlight row 1 of A and column 1 of B to compute the first entry of AB.
Then highlight row 1 of B and column 1 of A to show BA is a different product.
Circle the sizes: AB is 2×2 while BA is 3×3.
visualSpec.accuracyNote: The matrix products and sizes are exact.

walkthrough.label: Check sizes
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: The inner dimensions match for AB, so AB is defined and will be 2 by 2.

walkthrough.label: Compute \(AB\)
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Each entry is a row of A dotted with a column of B.

walkthrough.label: Check sizes for \(BA\)
walkthrough.latex: B\text{ is }3\times2,\quad A\text{ is }2\times3,\quad 	ext{so }BA\text{ is }3\times3.
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: This product is also defined, but it has a different size.

walkthrough.label: Compute \(BA\)
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Same rule, but the rows and columns come from different matrices now.

walkthrough.label: Compare
walkthrough.latex: AB\text{ is }2\times2,\quad BA\text{ is }3\times3,\quad 	ext{so }AB\ne BA.
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: They cannot be equal because their sizes are different.

commonMistakes: Multiplying entries in the same position instead of row dot column.
Assuming AB and BA should match.
Forgetting that AB and BA can have different sizes.
problems.practiceLabPointer: Practice Lab Section 1: matrix operations and multiplication.

problems.title: Transpose of a product reverses the order
problems.studentPromptLatex: Let \(A=\begin{bmatrix}1&-3&2\\0&4&-1\end{bmatrix}\) and \(C=\begin{bmatrix}2&0\\-1&5\\3&1\end{bmatrix}\). Compute \(AC\), then verify that \((AC)^T=C^TA^T\).
problems.whatToSayBefore: This problem is about order. The transpose of a product reverses the order.

visualSpec.purpose: Show that the product AC is 2×2 and that transposing the product matches C^T A^T.
boardSketchInstructions: Write \((AC)^T\) on the left and \(C^TA^T\) on the right.
Draw a small arrow showing that transpose flips the product order.
Compute AC first, then transpose it.
Then compute C^T A^T and show the same matrix appears.
visualSpec.accuracyNote: All matrices in this visual are exact.

walkthrough.label: Check the product size
walkthrough.latex: A\text{ is }2\times3,\quad C\text{ is }3\times2,\quad 	ext{so }AC\text{ is }2\times2.
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: The inner 3s match, so the product is allowed.

walkthrough.label: Compute \(AC\)
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Again, row dot column for each entry.

walkthrough.label: Compute \(C^T\) and \(A^T\)
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Notice the order is C transpose first, then A transpose.

walkthrough.label: Multiply \(C^TA^T\)
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: This matches \((AC)^T\), so the rule works in this example.

commonMistakes: Changing signs during the transpose. Transpose only flips positions; it does not change signs.
problems.practiceLabPointer: Practice Lab Section 1: transposes and matrix products.

problems.title: Use a matrix power pattern instead of multiplying forever
problems.studentPromptLatex: Let \(A=\begin{bmatrix}1&2\\0&-1\end{bmatrix}\). Compute \(A^2\). Then use that result to simplify \(A^{25}+3A^4-2I\).
problems.whatToSayBefore: Do not try to multiply A twenty-five times. First find a pattern.

visualSpec.purpose: Show the alternating pattern created by A^2=I.
patternFacts: Every odd positive power is A.
boardSketchInstructions: Compute A^2 slowly on the board.
When A^2=I appears, stop and write the pattern.
Write: even powers → I, odd powers → A.
visualSpec.accuracyNote: The power pattern is exact because A^2=I.

walkthrough.label: Compute \(A^2\)
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: This is the key moment. Once A squared equals I, powers become easy.

walkthrough.label: Use the pattern
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Odd power gives A. Even power gives I.

walkthrough.label: Substitute into the expression
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Combine the identity terms just like scalars because they are the same matrix.

commonMistakes: Squaring each entry instead of computing \(AA\).
Continuing to multiply many times instead of using \(A^2=I\).
Forgetting that \(I\) must be the correct size, here \(2\times2\).
problems.practiceLabPointer: Practice Lab Section 1: matrix powers and special matrices.

wrapUpQuestions: Before multiplying two matrices, what dimensions do you check?
What does one entry of AB come from?
Is AB usually equal to BA?
What is the correct formula for \((AB)^T\)?
What does \(A^2\) mean for a matrix?

linksToWebsite.label: Practice Lab Section 1
linksToWebsite.description: Use this section for more vector, matrix, transpose, and basic operation practice.

qualityControlNotes: All matrix products were checked by hand.
Problem 1: A is 2×3 and B is 3×2, so AB is 2×2 and BA is 3×3; both products are computed explicitly.
Problem 3 uses \(A^2=I\), so odd powers equal A and even powers equal I.
Visual specs are designed to show exact matrix structure and board-friendly highlights.
